author: @mas9510
text: I got The artic silver 5 thermal paste and mixed it with really fine diamond dust(<1 micron) with made paste really dense. But it brought my i9-9900k in cpuz test from 80-85c to 70-73c. Really did help in my opinion.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: That is awesome mate! Is that idle temps or under load temps because that is fantastic! :)

author: @William_Hada

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
most likely reason for better results is that your paste is thinning down from the vibrations and constant pressure on the paste to squeeze it out from between the heat sink and the processor, which is the optimum thing you really want, as the thermal paste should only be in the voids that with out it would be filled with air, and thus barriers to heat passage, and in your case it seems the paste is not drying out yet so it is able to make this transition, the curious thing will be if your paste can avoid the dry out effect that causes cracking and such in the unavoidable layer that will still remain permanently no matter what due to the solids in the paste that will not move out of the areas it is not really needed, due to creeping of the material (like a heavy bridge sitting on washers made of lead to deaden impact, after years it will have squeezed out the sides of the two joining points but all of it can never fully squeeze out) anyway since there is a flow component in the paste if this dries out it leaves a void causing shrinkage and due to lack of something to allow the solids to slide around it will end up cracking and pulling back leaving air voids in nearly all thermal paste, and people just reapply after cleaning normally (MX-4 paste is supposed to have the longest lifetime with out this happening but we will see as I have it in my two computers now ;) 

I wonder what other compounds you guys could put together to be a better thermal paste solid like how Robert made that Graphitic Carbon Nitride in the microwave ages ago, and later showed how it can be modified with ferric chloride, as I know boron nitride is used as a lubricant in place of graphite powder, and as a thermal paste now in hot ends for 3D printers, (could be something else you could stress test your paste formulas on too, to get faster stress testing due to higher temps when they are hot, because air voids and such slowing the rate of thermal dissipation into the nozzle is something that slows down 3D printing a bit, (and a lot once I get my 100 to 1000 nozzle at once system together for increasing printer speeds for plastic filament printing to lower say a 3 hour print down to less than 15 seconds or less LOL going to have to heat up a LOT of plastic in a VERY fast pace and short period of time, on the level of injection molding speeds XD) 

anyway a better thermal compound using something like BN is interesting since it is not a straight out conductor, and might be able to enable creep as the liquid or paste additive to keep it sticky and able to squish, is dried out leaving behind just the BN that is a lube anyway, so that maybe it can then continue moving around to let the pressure bring the parts together anywhere they can meet possible when pressed, 

Something I could see you and Robert collaborating directly on and you do some videos on the process if not both of you being in the video at once, though that does mean someone else has to run the camera or something I think LOL (time to get a raspberry pi RC wifi car with a webcam and pan tilt running so someone can run it remotely from somewhere else in the world :)

author: @OverUnity7734
text:
Once, in an emergency situation, I was able to use tooth paste as heat sink compound. 
It ran fine for the two weeks that it took for the Arctic Silver  to arrive .

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Haha I have heard of people using it in a quick emergency, does it really work well? If so that is awesome! :)

replies.author: @ScarletFlames1
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey Anything that bridges the microscopic gaps in the metal surfaces works, it's just that thermal paste has conductive particles in it suspended in the non-conductive gel solution in order to not short circuit if it splashes on the MOBO AND conduct heat really well as things that conduct heat really well usually conduct electricity too.

author: @kreynolds1123
text: its good that you include the ambient  temp. unfortunately  in 1006 you either didn't get or just didn't state the ambient.  what's important  is the  stress temp - ambient temp = over ambient temp.
